content.text: Retrieval is the step that turns a query vector into a ranked list of document chunks. It is the core of what makes RAG work: given millions of indexed passages, find the handful most relevant to the user's question in milliseconds. The dominant approach is Approximate Nearest Neighbour (ANN) search over a vector index, sometimes combined with metadata filtering to constrain the candidate set.

attrs.alt: Three panels: (1) ANN search in 2D vector space showing query point q surrounded by a top-k radius with document clusters; (2) similarity metrics — cosine, dot product, Euclidean; (3) LangChain retrieval modes. Bottom panel: pre-filter vs post-filter metadata strategies.

content.text: ANN Search: Why Not Exact Search?

An exact nearest-neighbour search computes the similarity of the query vector against every document vector and returns the top k. For N = 1 000 000 vectors of dimension D = 1 536, that is 1.5 billion float multiplications per query — around 2 ms on a modern CPU but 200 ms if the index does not fit in L3 cache.
ANN algorithms trade a small accuracy loss (returning

(Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is the dominant algorithm: it builds a multi-layer proximity graph where each node links to its nearest neighbours at multiple scales. A query traverses the graph from the top (coarse) layer down to the bottom (fine) layer, visiting only a small fraction of nodes.
Well-tuned HNSW achieves recall@10 ≥ 0.97 with 10–20× fewer distance computations than brute force. The
parameter controls the search-time recall vs speed trade-off: higher
visits more nodes and achieves higher recall at the cost of more computation.
The choice of similarity metric must match how the embedding model was trained and how the index was built. You cannot change the metric after indexing without rebuilding.
Text retrieval — direction matters, not magnitude
Equivalent to cosine when vectors are L2-normalised

OpenAI's text embedding models produce L2-normalised vectors, so cosine similarity and dot product give identical rankings. Chroma defaults to L2 distance, which can produce different rankings for unnormalised vectors — always verify the metric when switching vector stores.
LangChain's vector store classes all implement a uniform retriever interface. The four main retrieval methods:

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma

embeddings  = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(embedding_function=embeddings)

# Retrieve top-4 chunks most similar to the query
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    query="What was Q3 2025 cloud revenue?",
    k=4,
)
for doc in results:
    print(doc.metadata.get("source", "?"), "|", doc.page_content[:80])

# Returns list of (Document, float) tuples
results_with_scores = vectorstore.similarity_search_with_relevance_scores(
    query="What was Q3 2025 cloud revenue?",
    k=4,
)

for doc, score in results_with_scores:
    print(f"{score:.3f}  {doc.page_content[:60]}")
    # 0.872  Q3 2025 cloud revenue grew 34%...
    # 0.764  Enterprise segment added 2400 logos...
    # 0.521  R&D spending increased 22%...
    # 0.398  Weather in London was warm in October...

# Apply a score threshold to drop low-quality hits
THRESHOLD = 0.6
quality_results = [doc for doc, score in results_with_scores if score >= THRESHOLD]
Standard similarity search can return k near-duplicate chunks when the query matches a passage repeated across the corpus. MMR re-ranks retrieved candidates to maximise both relevance and diversity:
is the set of already-selected documents and

# fetch_k: initial candidate pool size (larger = more diversity options)
# k:       final number of results returned
results_mmr = vectorstore.max_marginal_relevance_search(
    query="What was Q3 2025 cloud revenue?",
    k=4,
    fetch_k=20,       # retrieve 20 candidates, pick 4 diverse ones
    lambda_mult=0.6,  # 0.6 = lean towards relevance; 0.5 = balanced
)

Use MMR when the corpus has many near-duplicate chunks (e.g., a document repeated in multiple formats) or when you need the context window to cover different aspects of a topic rather than hammer the same fact from multiple angles.
When documents carry metadata (source, date, document type, department), you can narrow the search to a subset before or after ANN scoring.

# Retrieve a larger k, then filter in Python
raw = vectorstore.similarity_search_with_relevance_scores(
    query="cloud revenue growth",
    k=20,   # over-retrieve
)
filtered = [
    doc for doc, score in raw
    if doc.metadata.get("year") == 2025
    and doc.metadata.get("doc_type") == "earnings_release"
    and score >= 0.5
][:4]   # cap at desired k
interface decouples the retrieval strategy from the rest of the pipeline. Any class implementing
is a retriever. This lets you swap in BM25, a web search API, or an ensemble without changing the chain.

# Wrap any vector store as a retriever
retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(
    search_type="mmr",         # "similarity" | "mmr" | "similarity_score_threshold"
    search_kwargs={
        "k": 5,
        "fetch_k": 20,         # used by MMR
        "lambda_mult": 0.6,
        "score_threshold": 0.5,  # used by similarity_score_threshold
    },
)

# Invoke the retriever directly
docs = retriever.invoke("What was Q3 2025 cloud revenue?")

# Or use it inside a chain
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Answer using only the provided context.\n\nContext:\n{context}"),
    ("human",  "{question}"),
])
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")

def format_docs(docs):
    return "\n\n".join(d.page_content for d in docs)

chain = (
    {"context": retriever | format_docs, "question": RunnablePassthrough()}
    | prompt
    | llm
)
answer = chain.invoke("What was Q3 2025 cloud revenue?")
print(answer.content)
The number of chunks to retrieve (
) directly affects both answer quality and cost:
The answer may be in chunk k+1; recall suffers.
The LLM context fills with irrelevant text; the signal-to-noise ratio drops; cost and latency increase.
Leave 30–40% of the context window for the system prompt, conversation history, and the LLM's generated answer. The remaining tokens are available for retrieved chunks.

ANN retrieval optimises for speed, not perfect ranking. A cross-encoder reranker reads the query and each retrieved chunk together (as a single pair) and scores relevance more accurately than a bi-encoder. Run it on the top-K results from ANN and re-rank before passing to the LLM:

from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

cross_encoder = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")

query = "What was Q3 2025 cloud revenue?"
candidates = retriever.invoke(query)   # ANN top-k (e.g. k=20)

# Score (query, passage) pairs
pairs  = [(query, doc.page_content) for doc in candidates]
scores = cross_encoder.predict(pairs)

# Sort by cross-encoder score, keep top-5
reranked = sorted(
    zip(candidates, scores),
    key=lambda x: x[1],
    reverse=True,
)[:5]

for doc, score in reranked:
    print(f"{score:.3f}  {doc.page_content[:60]}")
A cross-encoder runs in O(k) forward passes, not O(N), so it is only affordable because k ≪ N. Common open-source rerankers:
When users phrase queries naturally — "Show me earnings documents from Q3 2025 about cloud" — they embed both a semantic search term
a metadata constraint in a single sentence. A
uses an LLM to extract the structured filter from the natural language query before running the ANN search:

from langchain.retrievers.self_query.base import SelfQueryRetriever
from langchain.chains.query_constructor.schema import AttributeInfo
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

# Describe the metadata schema so the LLM knows what to extract
metadata_field_info = [
    AttributeInfo(name="doc_type",  description="Type of document", type="string"),
    AttributeInfo(name="year",      description="Publication year",  type="integer"),
    AttributeInfo(name="quarter",   description="Fiscal quarter (Q1–Q4)", type="string"),
    AttributeInfo(name="source",    description="Source filename",   type="string"),
]

embeddings  = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(embedding_function=embeddings)
llm         = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

retriever = SelfQueryRetriever.from_llm(
    llm=llm,
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    document_contents="Quarterly earnings report passages",
    metadata_field_info=metadata_field_info,
    verbose=True,   # shows the extracted query + filter
)

# The LLM extracts: semantic query = "cloud revenue", filter = {year: 2025, quarter: "Q3"}
results = retriever.invoke("cloud revenue in Q3 2025 earnings")
internally builds a structured query object from the natural language input using the LLM, then applies it as a pre-filter to the vector store. The
flag prints the extracted filter so you can verify correctness during development.
In a FastAPI or async web server, blocking retrieval calls hold the event loop. Use the async retriever interface for all production deployments:

import asyncio
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma

embeddings  = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(embedding_function=embeddings)
retriever   = vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 5})

async def retrieve(query: str):
    return await retriever.ainvoke(query)

# In a FastAPI handler:
# @app.post("/search")
# async def search(req: SearchRequest):
#     docs = await retriever.ainvoke(req.query)
#     return [d.page_content for d in docs]

# Concurrent retrieval for multiple queries (e.g. batch eval):
async def retrieve_batch(queries: list[str]):
    tasks = [retriever.ainvoke(q) for q in queries]
    return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
Retrieval quality is measured separately from answer quality. The key metrics:

attrs.latex: \frac{\text{queries where correct chunk is in top-}k}{\text{total queries}}

content.text: Did we retrieve the answer at all?
How high does the correct chunk rank?
Graded relevance accounting for rank position
Fraction of ground-truth answer attributable to retrieved chunks

from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import context_recall, context_precision
from datasets import Dataset

# Evaluation dataset: list of queries with ground-truth answers
# and the chunks retrieved for each query
data = {
    "question":    ["What was Q3 2025 cloud revenue?", "How much did R&D grow?"],
    "contexts":    [
        # list of chunk texts retrieved for each question
        ["Q3 cloud revenue grew 34%...", "Enterprise added 2400 logos..."],
        ["R&D spending increased 22%...", "Two new AI models shipped..."],
    ],
    "ground_truth": ["$2.1B, up 34% year-over-year", "22% increase"],
}
ds = Dataset.from_dict(data)

result = evaluate(ds, metrics=[context_recall, context_precision])
print(result)
# {'context_recall': 0.91, 'context_precision': 0.78}

Run this evaluation at multiple values of k (4, 8, 12) and with/without reranking and filters. Retrieval quality is usually the biggest lever on overall RAG accuracy — a 10% improvement in context recall translates almost directly into 10% better answer quality.

Large organisations often maintain separate indexes for different document types — one for regulatory filings, one for internal wikis, one for support tickets. Rather than merging everything into a single massive index (which degrades filtering precision), you can fan out a query across multiple retrievers and merge the results:

import asyncio
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# Each index holds a different document type
regulatory_store = Chroma(collection_name="regulatory", embedding_function=embeddings)
internal_store   = Chroma(collection_name="internal",   embedding_function=embeddings)
support_store    = Chroma(collection_name="support",    embedding_function=embeddings)

RETRIEVERS = [
    regulatory_store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 3}),
    internal_store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 3}),
    support_store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 3}),
]

async def multi_index_retrieve(query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[Document]:
    # Fan out to all indexes concurrently
    results_per_index = await asyncio.gather(
        *[r.ainvoke(query) for r in RETRIEVERS]
    )

    # Flatten and deduplicate by content hash
    seen, merged = set(), []
    for results in results_per_index:
        for doc in results:
            sig = hash(doc.page_content[:200])
            if sig not in seen:
                seen.add(sig)
                merged.append(doc)

    # Return up to top_k — for better ranking, apply a reranker here
    return merged[:top_k]

The fan-out is async so all three indexes are queried in parallel. The latency is that of the slowest index, not the sum. For regulated environments this approach also simplifies access control: each index can have its own permission boundary, and queries only fan out to indexes the user has access to.
Using the default k=4 for all use cases
Profile context window and chunk size; set k based on available tokens
Not filtering when the corpus spans multiple tenants or time ranges
Always filter on doc_type, date range, or tenant ID for production
Incorrect rankings; cosine and L2 disagree on unnormalised vectors
Fix metric at index time; rebuild index when changing
Passing raw retrieval results to the LLM without deduplication
Context window wastes tokens on repeated passages
Dedup by content hash before assembling context
Skipping reranking on a large or noisy corpus
Top retrieved chunk may not be the most relevant for the question
Add a cross-encoder reranker after ANN retrieval
